summary: I started working at Armour and Company after a year of trying to find office work downtown. In the early 1900s, my friend and I walked into Armour's hiring office just for fun, not expecting to get a job because we thought the yards were awful. Miss McCann picked me out and I was put to work in the Dry Casings department, which wasn’t as bad as the Gut Shanty but still tough with the pickle water and strong smells. I operated a power sewing machine there and earned between $23 and $27 a week, working about eight months a year for five years. My husband also worked there for thirty years but got sick from the conditions and was eventually laid off after being transferred to a new department.
